text: Minimum Standards for Prevention and Response to Gender-Based Violence in Emergencies

These minimum standards define the essential elements of GBV prevention and response programming in humanitarian emergencies. They are intended for all humanitarian actors — not only specialist GBV organisations — since all sectors have a responsibility to prevent GBV and ensure survivors can access care. The standards apply in the acute phase and throughout the emergency response.

GBV is a serious violation of human rights and a public health emergency. In humanitarian settings, GBV — including sexual violence, intimate partner violence, child marriage, sexual exploitation and abuse, and trafficking — increases substantially. Every humanitarian response must address GBV from day one, regardless of whether data yet exists to document its scale.
Standard 1: Coordination

GBV prevention and response programming must be coordinated through a multisectoral mechanism. In large-scale emergencies, this is typically the GBV Area of Responsibility (GBV AoR) or GBV sub-cluster, co-led by UNFPA and UNHCR. At field level, a GBV coordination group brings together all actors providing GBV services: health, psychosocial, legal, shelter, protection, and camp management.

The coordination body must: (1) map all GBV services and identify gaps, (2) develop and maintain a GBV referral pathway, (3) set minimum standards for safe and ethical data collection, (4) monitor and report on the GBV response, (5) advocate to other clusters (health, WASH, shelter) to integrate GBV risk mitigation into their programming.
Standard 2: Human Resources

GBV programme staff must be adequately trained, supervised, and supported. Burnout and secondary traumatic stress are serious risks for GBV caseworkers and health staff. Organisations must provide: regular supervision and debriefing, clear protocols for self-care, and appropriate caseload limits. GBV caseworkers should not carry more than 15–20 active cases simultaneously.

Staff training must cover: survivor-centred principles, confidentiality and ethical information sharing, basic psychosocial support, mandatory reporting obligations (context-specific), safety planning, and referral protocols. Training alone is insufficient — ongoing supervision and mentorship are essential to maintain quality.
Standard 3: Prevention

GBV prevention programming addresses the root causes and risk factors for GBV: gender inequality, social norms that condone violence, lack of rule of law, and breakdown of protective social networks. In humanitarian settings, risk factors are heightened by displacement, overcrowding, loss of livelihoods, alcohol abuse, and breakdown of community structures.

Prevention activities include: community awareness-raising with women, men, girls, and boys; engaging men as champions for gender equality; working with community leaders to condemn GBV; providing safe spaces for women and girls; supporting economic empowerment activities; and advocacy for legal protection.
Standard 4: Risk Mitigation

GBV risk mitigation requires all humanitarian sectors to reduce the risks of GBV inherent in their programmes. This is a responsibility of ALL clusters, not only the GBV sector. Examples: WASH — separate and lockable latrines for women, well-lit pathways, fuel for cooking to reduce firewood collection risk; Shelter — separate sleeping areas for men and women who are not family members, locks on shelter doors; Food — distribution systems that do not require women to walk alone at night.

The GBV coordination body should conduct a GBV risk analysis for each camp or displacement setting, identifying where and when risk is highest and working with other sectors to reduce it. Risk analysis should involve affected women and girls — they know their own risks best.
Standard 5: Health Response to GBV

All health facilities must provide clinical management of rape within 72 hours of assault. The clinical package includes: thorough assessment and documentation of injuries, emergency contraception (within 120 hours), HIV post-exposure prophylaxis (within 72 hours), STI prophylaxis, hepatitis B vaccination, and basic wound care. Psychological first aid must accompany all clinical care.

All clinical care for GBV survivors must be survivor-centred: the survivor decides what services to receive, when, and by whom. Informed consent is required for every intervention. Confidentiality must be maintained — medical records locked and information shared only with survivor consent. Do not involve family members without survivor request.

Health staff at all levels must be trained in survivor-centred principles. A survivor who is met with disbelief, blame, or insensitivity is less likely to return for follow-up care and less likely to report to authorities. The response of the first health worker a survivor encounters is critical to their recovery journey.
Standard 6: Psychosocial Support

GBV survivors require psychosocial support at all stages of recovery. In the immediate aftermath, psychological first aid (PFA) — not formal counselling — is appropriate. PFA involves: providing a safe space, listening without judgement, offering practical help, connecting to support networks, and linking to information and services. Trained community health workers can provide PFA.

Structured psychosocial support (group or individual) is appropriate once a survivor has stabilised. Case management — a structured process of identifying needs, developing a safety plan, and supporting access to services — is the backbone of the GBV response. Each survivor should have an assigned caseworker who coordinates their support across sectors.
Standard 7: Legal and Justice Response

Survivors of GBV should be informed of their legal options and supported to exercise them if they choose. Choices belong to the survivor — legal action must never be required to access services. Where legal protection systems function, survivors can be referred to: police, prosecutors, legal aid organisations, and formal justice mechanisms. Where formal justice is weak or harmful, community-based protection mechanisms may be more appropriate.

Documentation of GBV is critical for legal proceedings. The medical report is often key evidence. Health staff must be trained in forensic documentation — recording injuries accurately, preserving evidence, and understanding chain of custody. All documentation must be stored securely. A survivor may decide to pursue legal action months or years after the assault — documentation must be preserved.
Standard 8: Safety and Security

Physical safety of survivors, caseworkers, and community members is a prerequisite for effective GBV programming. Safety planning — developing a personalised plan for what a survivor will do if threatened — is a core case management activity. Safety plans must be realistic, confidential, and updated regularly.

Organisational safety measures include: secure storage of case files (locked, password-protected digital records), protocols for safe transportation of staff, safety audits of service delivery points, and incident reporting mechanisms. Never place a survivor at greater risk through programming activities — for example, by bringing her back to the community where the perpetrator lives without safety planning.
Standard 9: Case Management

GBV case management is a structured, multi-step process: (1) intake and assessment, (2) safety planning, (3) developing an action plan with the survivor, (4) providing direct support, (5) referral to other services, (6) follow-up, and (7) case closure. The process is driven by the survivor's priorities and decisions — not by the caseworker's judgement of what is best.

Ethical case management requires: (1) Informed consent — explain what information will be collected, how it will be stored, and who may access it. (2) Confidentiality — only share information with survivor consent, except when required by mandatory reporting laws. (3) Non-discrimination — do not refuse services based on age, sex, ethnicity, disability, or legal status. (4) Do no harm — avoid actions that could put the survivor or caseworker at greater risk.
Standard 10: Information Management

GBV data must be collected, stored, and shared in ways that protect survivor safety and confidentiality. The GBV Information Management System (GBV IMS) — a standardised data collection and reporting tool — is recommended for all GBV programmes. Individual-level case data must never be shared outside the organisation without survivor consent. Aggregate data (number of cases by type, age, sex) can be shared for coordination and advocacy.

Minimum data to collect: incident type, relationship of perpetrator to survivor, age and sex of survivor, date of incident, date of first service contact, services received. Do not collect names or identifying information in shared databases. De-identification is essential — any data that could be used to identify a survivor must be protected at the highest level.

A note on under-reporting: GBV is massively under-reported in all settings, including humanitarian emergencies. Absence of reported cases does not mean absence of GBV. Programmes must be designed assuming GBV is occurring and that survivors face barriers to reporting, including fear, shame, lack of knowledge of services, and distrust of authorities.
